Comparative experiments show that optimized messages achieve higher consistency and readability compared to automatically generated counterparts. The results indicate that leveraging human-written commit messages through optimization provides a more reliable and practical solution for commit message improvement in real-world software development environments.

Title: Seeing Through AI's Lens: Enhancing Human Skepticism Towards LLM-Generated Fake News
abstract: This paper examines methods for enhancing human skepticism toward fake news generated by large language models (LLMs). The study analyzes how exposure to AI-generated content characteristics influences users’ ability to identify misinformation. Through controlled experiments, participants evaluate news articles produced by LLMs under different disclosure and explanation settings. The results indicate that increasing transparency about generative mechanisms and highlighting linguistic cues associated with synthetic text significantly improves users’ skepticism and detection accuracy. The findings suggest that human-centered interventions can mitigate the risks posed by LLM-generated fake news and support more resilient information consumption practices.The rapid advancement of large language models has increased the realism and scalability of fake news generation. This research investigates strategies to strengthen human skepticism toward LLM-generated misinformation. By comparing user responses to AI-generated and human-written news articles, the study evaluates the impact of awareness cues and analytical guidance on misinformation detection. Experimental results show that informed users demonstrate higher skepticism and improved judgment accuracy when assessing AI-generated fake news. The study contributes empirical insights into combating AI-driven misinformation and informs the design of effective countermeasures for digital media literacy.

Title: Contrasting Linguistic Patterns in Human and LLM-Generated News Text
abstract: This investigation analyzes linguistic differences between human-authored news articles and texts generated by large language models (LLMs). Outputs from six LLMs across three model families (LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon) and varying parameter sizes are compared to human-written English news from contemporary sources. Multiple linguistic dimensions are evaluated, including morphological richness, syntactic complexity, dependency structures, constituent lengths, sentence length variation, lexical diversity metrics (STTR, MTLD), psychometric properties (emotion, toxicity), and sociolinguistic biases (sexism). Results demonstrate systematic contrasts: human texts exhibit greater vocabulary richness, scattered sentence lengths, optimized dependency distances, stronger negative emotions, and inherent biases. LLM-generated texts display increased use of numbers, symbols, auxiliaries, pronouns, and heightened toxicity in larger models. Implications for AI detection and bias mitigation are examined.Large language models (LLMs) produce news-like text, yet alignment with human journalistic patterns remains unestablished. This study contrasts linguistic features in human-written news against outputs from multiple LLMs (LLaMA 7B-70B, Mistral 7B, Falcon 7B-180B) prompted with recent headlines. Analyses cover syntactic parsing (dependencies, constituents), morphological patterns, lexical diversity (type-token ratios, MTLD), sentence variability, emotional valence, toxicity levels, and gender bias indicators. Findings reveal human texts feature higher lexical diversity, diverse dependency types, shorter constituents, negative emotional dominance, and moderate biases. LLM texts show uniform sentence structures, objective markers (numerals, auxiliaries), reduced negative affect, amplified toxicity with scale, and exaggerated sexist patterns in most models. Differences exceed inter-LLM variation. Outcomes inform forensic linguistics and ethical AI deployment.

Title: Unleashing the power of text for credit default prediction: Comparing human-written and generative AI-refined texts
abstract: This study investigates the effectiveness of textual information for credit default prediction by comparing human-written texts with generative AI-refined texts. Natural language processing techniques are applied to extract linguistic and semantic features from both text types, which are then integrated into predictive credit risk models. Model performance is evaluated using standard classification metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC. Experimental results indicate that generative AI-refined texts enhance feature consistency and improve predictive performance compared to raw human-written texts. The findings demonstrate the potential of generative AI as a tool for refining textual data to support more accurate and robust credit default prediction models.Textual data has become an important source of information for credit risk assessment. This paper examines the impact of generative AI-refined text on credit default prediction performance in comparison with human-authored text. Using a supervised learning framework, textual representations derived from both text sources are incorporated into credit scoring models. The comparative analysis shows that AI-refined texts reduce noise and improve model generalization while preserving essential semantic content. The results suggest that generative AI can effectively augment human-written text for financial risk modeling, offering practical implications for data-driven credit assessment systems.

Title: Do LLMs produce texts with "human-like" lexical diversity?
abstract: This study examines lexical diversity patterns in texts generated by large language models (LLMs). Texts produced by multiple GPT variants are compared to human-written samples from native and non-native English speakers across varying proficiency levels. Six dimensions of lexical diversity are analyzed: volume, variety-repetition ratio, evenness, disparity, dispersion, and abundance. Results show systematic deviations in LLM outputs, with higher uniformity in word distribution and elevated unique word usage relative to text length. Newer models exhibit greater divergence from human patterns. Implications for detecting machine-generated content and applications in linguistic analysis are discussed.Large language models (LLMs) generate coherent text, but the extent of human-like lexical diversity remains undetermined. This investigation compares outputs from four ChatGPT models against human texts collected from 240 participants representing L1 and L2 English users at different educational stages. Metrics applied include type-token ratios adjusted for length, hypergeometric distribution measures, and multi-dimensional indices capturing repetition, evenness, and dispersion. Findings indicate LLMs produce texts with hyper-diverse vocabulary profiles that exceed human norms in uniqueness and evenness while lacking natural repetition patterns. Advanced models display reduced similarity to human writing. These outcomes highlight limitations in mimicking authentic lexical sophistication.

Title: Studying How Configurations Impact Code Generation in LLMs: the Case of ChatGPT
abstract: This paper investigates the impact of configuration parameters on code generation performance in large language models, with a specific focus on ChatGPT. Different configuration settings, including temperature, prompt structure, and system-level constraints, are systematically analyzed to evaluate their influence on code correctness, readability, and efficiency. Experimental results demonstrate that variations in configurations significantly affect the syntactic accuracy and logical consistency of generated code. The findings highlight that lower-temperature configurations tend to produce more deterministic and structurally correct outputs, while higher-temperature settings increase diversity at the cost of reliability. This study provides empirical evidence that configuration tuning is a critical factor in optimizing code generation tasks and offers insights for researchers and practitioners aiming to improve automated programming systems. Large language models have shown strong capabilities in automated code generation; however, the role of configuration parameters in shaping output quality remains underexplored. This study examines how different configuration settings influence code generation behavior in ChatGPT. By conducting controlled experiments across multiple programming tasks, the research evaluates variations in functional correctness, error frequency, and response consistency under different configurations. The results indicate that configuration choices directly impact model performance and output stability. The analysis confirms that carefully selected configurations can significantly enhance code generation reliability. These findings contribute to a deeper understanding of configuration-sensitive behavior in large language models and support the development of more robust AI-assisted software engineering workflows.
